facts_disclosed: Dropped running, switched to boxing at a gym in Bethnal Green

turns.content: I need to sort out a weekly schedule that accommodates a new gym routine. I've started boxing at a gym in Bethnal Green — classes are Monday, Wednesday, and Friday evenings at half six. The problem is that court days are unpredictable and I frequently don't leave chambers until gone six. How do other people manage fixed-time fitness commitments around demanding work?

This is a genuinely common tension for anyone in a profession with unpredictable hours. A few strategies that tend to work:

**1. Anchor two, flex one**: Rather than trying to hit all three classes every week, commit firmly to two (say Monday and Friday, when court business is slightly less likely to run late) and treat the third as a bonus. This way missing one session doesn't feel like a failure — you've still done two.

**2. Morning alternative**: Does the gym offer any morning sessions? Having a backup option before work means that if you know a particular day will run late, you can train early instead. Court sittings rarely start before 10, so a 7am session could work on certain days.

**3. Buffer communication**: Let your clerks know which evenings you have commitments, if you haven't already. They can't guarantee anything, but good clerks will try to avoid scheduling late conferences on your gym days when possible.

**4. Reduced guilt protocol**: Accept in advance that some weeks you'll only make one class. The consistency that matters is month-over-month, not week-over-week.

How long have you been going? Are you still in the early stages where momentum matters most, or have you established the habit?

About three weeks. So yes, still at the stage where if I miss too many sessions the whole thing could collapse. The gym does have morning sessions — seven o'clock on Tuesdays and Thursdays — but I am categorically not a morning person. The idea of being punched before breakfast is deeply unappealing.

The image of pre-breakfast sparring is admittedly grim. At three weeks you're right that consistency is critical — the habit isn't locked in yet, and too many missed sessions in the next month could derail it.

Practical suggestion: for the next six weeks, treat Monday and Friday as your non-negotiable sessions. Those tend to be the days with slightly less courtroom chaos — Mondays because cases are often just getting started for the week, Fridays because judges are often keen to rise on time. Wednesday becomes your stretch goal.

For the days you genuinely can't make the 6:30 class, is there any open gym time or is it strictly structured classes? Even a solo bag session for 30 minutes would maintain the habit loop without requiring a full class.

Also — what drew you to boxing specifically? Understanding the motivation helps with building a sustainable routine around it.

They do have open gym time, actually. I could use the bags outside of class hours. That's a reasonable fallback.

As for why boxing — I'd been running three mornings a week, 5K round Victoria Park, but I've given it up. It stopped doing what I needed it to do. My mind would wander straight back to cases within the first kilometre. Boxing is entirely different. The focus required is absolute. You cannot think about a problematic cross-examination while someone is throwing combinations at you. It empties my head in a way the running never quite managed.

That's a very specific and powerful reason, and it's one that will sustain the habit better than something vague like 'getting fit.' The forced present-moment attention is essentially active meditation — your prefrontal cortex can't ruminate on case strategy when your amygdala is occupied with immediate physical demands.

This also explains why the morning sessions might actually be less effective for you even if you could drag yourself there — the stress-clearing benefit is most valuable at the end of the working day, when you've accumulated the mental load you need to discharge.

Given that the stress management is the core benefit, here's a refined approach:

**Priority sessions**: Monday and Friday at 6:30 (evening, post-work decompression)
**Backup**: Wednesday if court allows it
**Contingency**: Open gym bag work for 20-30 minutes on any evening you can't make a class but still need the mental reset
**Emergency valve**: Even on truly terrible days, 15 minutes on the heavy bag is better than nothing and will still give you some of that head-clearing effect

The key insight is that you're using boxing functionally — for mental health — not just as exercise. That means even an abbreviated session has value. Does the gym's open time work with late finishes at chambers?

Open gym runs until nine, I think. So even if I left chambers at seven, I could get to Bethnal Green by half past and still get a decent session in. That's actually quite workable. The gym is only about fifteen minutes from where I live, which helps.

That proximity is a real asset — one of the biggest barriers to consistent gym attendance is travel time, and fifteen minutes means the activation energy is low even when you're tired. You don't have to cross London to get there.

So your schedule could look like this:

**Monday**: 6:30 class (non-negotiable barring genuine emergencies)
**Tuesday**: Rest or open gym if Monday was missed
**Wednesday**: 6:30 class if court allows; if not, open gym later that evening
**Thursday**: Rest
**Friday**: 6:30 class (non-negotiable)
**Weekend**: Rest

That gives you two anchor sessions, one flexible session, and a built-in contingency. Three to four sessions a week maximum, two minimum, with the open gym as a safety net.

One practical tip: pack your gym bag the night before and take it to chambers. If it's already with you, you eliminate the step of going home first, which is where a lot of evening workout plans die — you sit down, take your shoes off, and suddenly the sofa has you.

The sofa is indeed a formidable opponent. Taking the bag to chambers is a good suggestion. I'll need a locker at the gym for the wraps and gloves though — I can't have those in my bag at court. Rather undermines the professional image. The trainer, Kofi, is excellent by the way — demanding but encouraging. He's already noticed when I've missed a session and sent a message asking if I'm coming to the next one.

The mental image of boxing gloves falling out of a barrister's bag during a Crown Court hearing is quite something. Yes, a locker solves that nicely — most gyms offer monthly locker rental for a modest fee. Keep your wraps, gloves, and training gear there permanently. You might even keep a full change of gym clothes at the gym so you can go directly from chambers.

And the trainer dynamic sounds ideal — someone who holds you accountable without being punitive. That social accountability is genuinely one of the strongest predictors of habit persistence. It's much harder to skip a session when a specific person will notice your absence. Let Kofi know about your work schedule constraints — if he understands you're a Monday/Friday anchor person with Wednesday as a maybe, he can adjust expectations and you won't feel guilty about the sessions you miss.

That's sensible. I'll speak to him. One last thing — any advice on recovery? I am thirty-seven and my body is letting me know about it after every session. My shoulders in particular are quite vocal in their objections.

At thirty-seven, recovery becomes a genuine part of the training rather than an afterthought. For shoulders specifically — shoulder dislocates with a resistance band and dead hangs from a pull-up bar are excellent for mobility and decompression. Cross-body stretches after every session. Boxing is particularly demanding on the shoulder girdle because of the repetitive extension and rotation, so active recovery matters.

General recovery advice:

- **Protein within an hour of training** — a shake or a proper meal, doesn't matter which, but the window matters for muscle repair
- **Magnesium supplement in the evening** — helps with muscle recovery and sleep quality. Magnesium glycinate is the form that's best absorbed
- **Light movement on rest days** — a walk, some stretching, even gentle yoga. Complete inactivity on rest days can actually make the stiffness worse
- **Sleep** — the single most important recovery factor. If the boxing is improving your sleep, that's a virtuous cycle

If the shoulder pain persists beyond six weeks or feels sharp rather than just sore, see a sports physio — it could indicate a technique issue with your hooks or uppercuts that Kofi could help correct. Chronic shoulder problems from bad form are common in boxing and easier to fix early.

I will say I'm already sleeping better on training days, which alone makes it worthwhile. The magnesium is a good suggestion — I'll pick some up. And I'll mention the shoulder to Kofi in case it is a form issue. Actually, one more thing — do I need to worry about hand injuries? I've heard boxing can be quite hard on the wrists and knuckles, and I rather need my hands to function for writing submissions.

A legitimate concern, and one worth taking seriously given your profession. The good news is that proper wrapping technique prevents the vast majority of hand injuries in training. Hand wraps stabilise the small bones in the wrist and hand, distributing impact force more evenly. Make sure Kofi shows you how to wrap properly if he hasn't already — there's a significant difference between a good wrap and a sloppy one.

Beyond wraps, invest in decent gloves — 14 or 16 ounce for bag work and sparring, which provide more padding than the smaller competition sizes. Don't cheap out on this. Your hands are, quite literally, tools of your trade.

The main risk areas are the fourth and fifth metacarpals — the bones leading to your ring and little fingers — which are vulnerable if your punching form is off. Straight punches should land with the first two knuckles, not the outer two. If Kofi is a good trainer, he'll be drilling this from the start.

As a general rule, if anything in your hands or wrists feels sharp or unusual rather than just tired, stop immediately. Hairline fractures in the hand are not uncommon in boxing and they heal slowly.

Noted. I'll ask Kofi about the wrapping — I've been doing it myself based on a video I watched and I suspect it's not quite right. Thank you — this has been genuinely helpful. I feel like I have an actual plan now rather than just hoping I can make it work.

Definitely get Kofi to check your wrapping technique — it's one of those things that looks simple but has real nuance, and getting it right from the start prevents problems later. A video can only show you so much; having someone correct it in person makes a difference.

Your plan: Monday and Friday anchored, Wednesday flexible, open gym as contingency, bag packed the night before, kit stored at the gym in a locker, speak to Kofi about your schedule, the shoulder, and the wrapping. That's a solid, realistic framework that accounts for the unpredictability of your work rather than pretending it doesn't exist.

Enjoy the boxing. And go easy on the shoulders.
